title: The agent-ready web: Simplify user actions with WebMCP — Tara Agyemang, Google
summary: WebMCP is a proposed web standard from Google that allows AI agents to interact with web applications through structured tools instead of DOM manipulation. It provides declarative HTML attributes for auto-generated schemas and an imperative JavaScript API for custom multi-step workflows.
writeup: A Google Chrome DevRel engineer introduces Web MCP (Web Model Context Protocol), a proposed browser standard that lets sites expose their capabilities as structured tools so in-browser AI agents can act reliably without scraping DOM trees, accessibility trees, or screenshots. The talk demonstrates the idea with a maze escape game driven entirely by Gemini 1.5 calling registered tools, then explains the two implementation paths: a declarative HTML attribute API for standard forms and an imperative JavaScript API for complex multi-step flows. The through-line is that the web was built for human eyes, and Web MCP is the minimal addition that makes existing sites legible to agents without requiring a full rewrite.
key_points: Unassisted browser agents today parse the full DOM, inspect the accessibility tree, take screenshots, and compute pixel coordinates before clicking, a process that is slow, brittle, and expensive in tokens, and can still fail if an ad shifts the layout after all that work.

Web MCP is a proposed web standard from the Chrome team that exposes a site's capabilities as a named, schema-described menu of tools; instead of guessing what a page does, an in-browser agent calls the tool directly, which the Chrome team describes as the 'USB-C of AI agent interactions.'

Web MCP is client-side only (the browser window must be open) and is inspired by but distinct from server-side MCP: it implements the tools portion of the MCP spec for in-browser agents, roughly analogous to how JavaScript is related to Java.

Good semantic HTML and robust accessibility standards are a prerequisite: the presenter explicitly states that agent-readiness follows from accessibility-first web foundations, and Web MCP only makes sense to add once those are in place.

The declarative API requires only a few HTML attributes (tool name, tool description) on a standard form element; the browser auto-generates the JSON schema the agent reads, and an agent-invoked boolean attribute lets the server distinguish human-submitted from agent-submitted forms.
The imperative API uses a registerTool JavaScript function with a manually authored JSON schema for complex, multi-step UI flows that cannot be captured by a single form element.
